title: Chapter 14
text: Chapter Eleven L ara entered the shower after leaving the attic, and Ronin banished himself to the lower level, doing all he could to keep from simulating images of her naked body glistening in the water . He moved his bag into the kitchen and unpacked the food, setting it out on the island counter. After refilling her canteen, he carried it to the table, seating himself. He removed the journal from his pocket. A human had penned it, just as Lara said, and Ronin knew whatever story it told would not be comforting to either of them. He brushed his thumb over the cover, but did not open it . After eleven minutes and fourteen seconds, Lara stepped into the kitchen. She wore some of the clothes he’d obtained for her — olive fatigues with patches on the knees and a baggy, off-white wool sweater. Its broad neck sagged off her shoulder. He averted his gaze as she hurried to the waiting food . Ronin watched her eat, thankful for the distraction. The eclectic meal she’d selected rapidly vanished, and he couldn’t look away from the subtle changes flickering over her expression with each new flavor, couldn’t look away from her tongue slipping out to lick her lips clean. She made soft, contented sounds in her throat as she chewed. Even in something as simple as food, she found pleasure. No programming required . “So,” she said around a mouthful of food, “Ronin your real name ?” “It’s the only one I have,” he replied, forcing his optics to meet her eyes . “Did someone give it to you, or did you choose it ?” “I chose it. Twelve years after I was reactivated .” “Twelve years with no name?” She swallowed, ran her tongue over her teeth, and took another bite . Though he was fully aware at the time, those years were still confusing to him, the memories somehow disorienting. Despite all the data he’d accumulated since, his recollection of that period was forever tainted by the memory of how little he understood at the time. He’d known the world was broken, that everything was wrong, but couldn’t determine how or why . “I didn’t need a name to identify myself .” “But you gave yourself one, anyway .” “For the benefit of others. Easier than saying that bot or hey you .” “Then why does everyone around here call you dustwalker ?” “Because there are few who choose to do as I do. It’s an easily identifiable title, and there are rarely more than one or two successful dustwalkers in any given town at any time. We’re…respected. But also, a little feared. Always…on the outside .” “Why is that?” Lara folded a slice of meat and laid it on her waiting tongue . “Bots operate on logic. Constantly assessing risk ver — ” “Isn’t it more logical not to go into the Dust ?” Ronin tilted his mouth into a smirk. “Yes. But settlements need raw materials to continue to produce the parts that bots need to function. Someone has to go out and find them .” “Why do you ?” “Should I be making a list of these questions so I can eventually answer them all, or do you just lose interest if my answer is more than a few words long ?” She chewed slowly, eyes wide as she stared at him. Seconds ticked by. The only sounds were Lara’s mastication and the refrigerator’s soft hum . “Getting a little irritated there, dustwalker ?” “I prefer you call me Ronin,” he replied . “ Why ?” He couldn’t tell for sure, but there seemed to be a hint of humor in her voice . “I think I’m through answering questions for now, human .” “Point taken .” “May I get back to what I was saying ?” She picked up the canteen, unscrewed the cap, and took a drink. Seeing her use it made him feel something close to satisfaction. Wiping her mouth with the back of her hand, she nodded . “You asked why there are so few dustwalkers. It’s because most who take up the calling are ended in the Dust. Whether a walker is a human or a bot, nobody goes to look for them when they don’t return .” The motion of her jaw slowed. “And…you go out there by choice ?” “Yes.” Because he was compelled to . “ Why ?” “Perhaps the risk-calculation function of my processors was damaged in the Black Out .” “But you know the risks. You said you were trying to survive, just like me. How is putting yourself in danger like that survival ?” Ronin lifted a hand and scratched his cheek. Touching it with something other than the bare metal of his fingers was nearly as strange as the impulse to scratch in the first place . “It’s survival because I’m still moving,” he finally said. “Existence is a constant fight, and I choose to seek that fight on my own terms. If I were to stay in a place like this all the time…at some point, I’d sit down and never get up again .” “Because you feel like you have no purpose .” Her words flared across his processors like a physical blow. A second passed; three seconds; fifteen. It was half a minute before he formulated a response . “I never said that .” “You didn’t have to.” She slid the food wrapper away, a half a strip of meat and three slices of carrot still atop it. “Why’d you pick Ronin ?” Electrodes crackled across his cheek again, demanding physical stimulation, but he kept his hand down. Was he so easily assessed? After all this time, was he so easy to read ? “In the twelfth year after I was awoken, I found a book. There were many more of them, back then. This one was about a place called Japan, which must have existed centuries ago. It spoke of elite warriors called samurai . They were the elite of their society, trained from young ages, feared by their enemies. But it was the ronin who caught my interest. In Japanese, it meant wave man . They were soldiers without masters, seeking causes worthy of their skill. Wandering the lengths of their land, bound by nothing but their blades and how far their feet could carry them. It…spoke to me .” “How much of it spoke to you ?” “Enough to choose Ronin as my name.” He dragged the paper closer and wrapped the remaining food. His chair stuttered over the floor as he stood up. Gathering the other food, he placed it all in the refrigerator and returned to his seat . Lara’s brow was furrowed, her eyes fixed on him. Her expression was thoughtful, but there were still hints of wariness in her posture . “You’re not like the other bots .” “Doubt either of us have spent enough time around other bots to know for certain .” “I’ve been around enough,” she replied. “You’re different .” “And you’re different from the other humans I’ve dealt with.” It was an understatement, but he couldn’t put his complex reasoning into words; he still didn’t quite comprehend why she was different . Her expression shifted again. It was another subtle change, but he was recognizing them in her with more ease, even if he didn’t know what they entailed . “You mentioned your sister,” he said when she made no reply. “Is there anyone else ?” Lara dropped her gaze to the table. “Don’t think so. I remember my mom being sick a lot, but she went scavenging every day, anyway. One time, she just never came back. I was five, I think. Never knew who my father was, if he was dead or alive. Don’t think mom did, either .” “Cheyenne doesn’t seem a good place for a five-year-old on her own.” What had she looked like at that age? How much had she changed since then ? She leaned back, settling a hand on her abdomen. “I was crying one day, because my stomach hurt. Mom always told me to suck it up, so I never cried when she was around. But this time, I couldn’t help it. It just hurt so much.” Frowning, she moved forward again, folding her arms and settling them on the edge of the table. “Tabitha found me and asked why I was crying. She was a few years older, and had already been on her own for about a year. She scavenged as best she could, and I think some of the adults took pity and helped her out sometimes . “So, anyway, she sat down next to me and hugged me because I just couldn’t stop crying, not even to answer her. She knew, though. Even without me saying anything, she knew I was alone and hungry. She held me, and, after a while, she gave me a potato from her pocket. That…that was all the food she had, just then. And she told me we were sisters, and that she’d always take care of me .” She smiled softly, moisture gathering in her eyes. “I’ll never forget that moment. I think…I know it was the first time I ever felt like someone cared about me. The first time I ever felt loved .” Lara’s smile fell away, and she met his gaze again, brows low. “I need to find her, Ronin. She’s all I have .” The layered emotions in her voice and expression were more than he could decipher. Fear, sorrow, determination, affection…loneliness? Solitude was familiar to Ronin; he’d wandered the Dust for so long by himself, never staying in one place for long, never building lasting relationships . What would it be like to have a companion, to share his existence with another person ? Would it be like this ? “I’ll do everything I can , Lara .” She’d told him bots did what they said, and he intended to. Not because of programming, not because it was the nature of his kind…but because he wanted to help her. His primary desire had long been to determine his true purpose, but his wants were increasingly building regarding this woman . She closed her eyes and eased back in the chair, exhaling. Relieved. Did that mean she’d placed some trust in him? That she had faith he would follow through ? “Right,” she said finally, opening her eyes. “Guess with all you’ve done, I’d better keep my end of the deal. We doing this in here ?” Images of Lara dancing in her shack flashed through his CPU, every movement framed forever by the slitted door through which he’d watched. Even if she performed the same dance now, he knew every step would be subtly different, somehow reflecting her current state of mind . “Where would you be most comfortable ?” “This is fine.” She rose quickly and walked to the relatively clear part of the kitchen, fingers trailing over the countertop. “So…you got any music ?” “No. I thought you made your own .” “Of course you’d remember that .” “I remember everything .” Her cheeks reddened before she looked away. Everything . The image of her on the bathroom floor returned, unbidden, and he knew his choice of words had been poor . “Okay, then.” Keeping her back to him, Lara inhaled deeply, shoulders rising and falling . She swayed her hips from side to side and lifted her arms over her head, grasping an elbow with each hand. Bowing her back, she turned gracefully to face him, bringing her hands down to slide over her chest, waist, and thighs. She kept her eyes closed . Arousal stirred within Ronin; though her clothing did nothing to accentuate her body, she was an attractive woman, and he longed to touch her so he could experience her through a different sensory input . Still, seconds ticked by as he awaited some more profound reaction in himself, awaited the deeper movement in his processors that had hit him the first time. This dance was familiar, but bore no resemblance to the one she’d performed in her shack; he’d seen this sort of movement at Kitty’s . Her body was moving for him, but she wasn’t dancing . This was closer to a maintenance bot mowing the grass in the park. Movement with purpose, but without feeling. The motions of an automaton . “No,” he said . She jumped, startled, and swung her eyes to him . “ What ?” “This isn’t what I want .” “What the hell do you mean?” The line between her brows returned; danger ahead . She threw her arms to the sides, palms up. “I’m dancing. That’s what you wanted !” “You are moving your parts — ” “Isn’t that what dancing is? Moving your body ?” Ronin pressed his lips into a tight line until she was done interrupting. “Dance like you did the night I first saw you .” “What do you mean? Like…the same dance ?” “It doesn’t matter if it’s the same. Just…” There were hundreds of thousands of words — if he only counted English — stored in his memory with full definitions, and he still couldn’t find the right ones to convey his meaning. “I don’t want you to dance like you’re on that stage. Dance like you would for yourself .” She stared at him with mild surprise and confusion. It seemed she’d stay that way indefinitely, until, after twenty-five seconds, she walked to the table, grabbed a chair, and dragged it to where she’d been standing . “ Fine .” The chair’s legs whined as she turned it so it was sideways to Ronin. Facing the counter, she settled her hands on the back of the chair, took another deep breath, and stilled . She tilted her head to the side, locking gazes with him, and twisted her hips. Her legs moved, their motion so graceful, so fluid, that she appeared to be walking on air. She tipped the chair onto one leg and spun it, off-balance, twice. When she stopped it’s motion, the seat faced Ronin . Sliding her fingers over its top, she moved around the chair and lay down over it, her hair brushing the floor. Her other hand traced a path from her neck down, avoiding the mound of her breasts as it ran over her ribs and down to her knee . She didn’t break eye contact as she drew her legs up one-at-a-time, pants sliding down to reveal shapely calves. Lowering her feet to the floor, she sat up, swinging a leg to straddle the chair . Sparks crackled over the surface of Ronin’s skin. Though she was fully clothed, Lara’s dance was the most sensual thing he’d ever seen. That his experience was limited in such matters should have tempered the realization, but made no difference. His lingering arousal rekindled, far stronger than before . Her hands fell to her knees, only to slide up again, this time into her hair. Elbows out to either side, she lifted her wild locks and undulated her stomach. Her breasts strained against the fabric of her sweater . How had he never noticed the similarities between dancing and sex ? She intensified the movements of her torso and, for a few seconds, thrashed like a caged animal. Rage and frustration flowed from her, and raw sexuality. She was a wild, untamable force, displaying her majesty just for him . Ronin’s awareness of the room around him — of the house, the district, the town, the entire world — faded away, all his attention focused solely on Lara . And she somehow kept her gaze on him throughout, her eyes burning with silent, sultry intensity like he’d never seen. Standing, she spun and kicked the chair away. Her body moved like a whirlwind and a stalking cat at once, a contradiction, an impossibility, and he could not look away . She fell to the floor like she’d been beaten, only to surge up again, stronger, and repeated the movements over and over, varying them each time. She closed her eyes, her expression full of feeling . It was a message to him. He knew it, though he did not yet understand the language of her body. She was ocean waves crashing against the shore; relentless, powerful, and fluid . Synths were capable of more speed, of more precision — Lara never performed a move the same way twice — but her flexibility, her fluidity, and the way she threw herself off balance but never lost it combined into something impossible for a bot. Ronin should have known exactly how she accomplished it . Comprehension remained elusive, however; could it be her emotions? They were more visible with each passing moment, written on her face, conveyed through the building drama of her dance . Had he been arrogant enough to assume he’d puzzle it all out after a dance or two? Emotion was the key, it had to be, and that only raised more questions . Lara dropped to her knees, head bowed, arms hanging to the sides. Her disheveled hair obscured her face. The refrigerator buzzed, oblivious to the rhythm of her quick, ragged breaths . Finally, she lifted her face, tugging the hair out of it. Her eyes sparkled above flushed cheeks . “That better?” she asked . “Yes. Thank you .” Her eyes narrowed. “You didn’t like it .” There was danger in her statement, but he couldn’t determine why . “It’s given me much to consider.” He liked it very much, but needed time to determine the reasons . “The fuck does that mean?” She stood, breathing heavily. His attention was drawn to her chest again . “You’ve fulfilled your obligation tonight. Will you be ready again tomorrow ?” “It’s not like I need charging or anything .” “Your people call it eating and sleeping .” “Are you dismissing me?” She folded her arms across her chest, settling her weight on one foot. The stance pressed her breasts together, pushing them up . No touching . “No. This is your residence now, as much as it’s mine. You’re welcome to be in whatever room you choose, whenever you want to.” He recalled the journal; perhaps not the attic again . “Yeah, well…I’m going to bed anyway .” “I’ve upset you again .” “Of course not.” Her tone suggested otherwise . “I know close to a million words in this language, by some counts, and I still don’t know the right ones to say to you .” She rolled her eyes and shifted her weight, cocking her hip in the other direction. The curve of her hip was just visible through her loose clothing . “I’m just tired, okay? So, uh, goodnight. Or whatever your kind says to each other .” “Goodnight’s fine. Sleep well , Lara .” When he reviewed the conversation afterward, he was certain she muttered something as she walked away, but he couldn’t make out the words. She didn’t look back when she reached the stairs . After he heard her bedroom door open and close, Ronin sat at the table and lost himself in her dance, repeatedly, as night trekked irreversibly towards the dawn .
